---
title: "About this example"
---

## Why this repository exists

This example is designed for teaching. It shows students how to organize a thesis or dissertation repository so that the logic of the project is visible from the first page.

A well-kept research repository should make three things easy to find:

1. what the project is about,
2. how the data were processed,
3. how the results and figures were produced.

## What students should notice

Students should notice that this repository separates:

- project explanation from analysis code,
- raw inputs from saved outputs,
- exploratory work from final figures.

That separation is one of the habits that makes research easier to review, revise, and defend.

## Adapting this for a real thesis

In a real thesis repository, the student would usually add:

- a fuller data dictionary,
- citations and references,
- rendered tables,
- manuscript or chapter text,
- and a short note on how others can reproduce the analysis.

## Teaching note

This site is intentionally modest. It is meant to model solid scholarly organization, not flashy design.
